content.text: When an agentic AI system degrades at 2 AM, the on-call engineer's first action should not be to figure out who to call. The contacts, the thresholds for escalation, and the runbook reference for every trigger type must be precomputed, codified, and reachable in under 30 seconds. Tiered escalation contact structures formalise this so that every incident progresses through a predictable sequence of increasingly senior responders at defined time boundaries.

A tiered escalation contact structure is a documented and tooling-enforced mapping from incident severity and type to the ordered sequence of responders who should be paged, and the time boundaries after which each tier should be escalated to the next. For agentic AI systems, the structure must account for AI-specific incident types that require specialist knowledge: LLM provider failures, prompt injection attacks, model output safety violations, and agent behaviour regressions that appear as business-logic failures rather than infrastructure failures.

The structure has four components: (1) a contact registry that maps tiers to named individuals, on-call rotation schedules, and contact methods; (2) runbooks that link each trigger type to a specific procedural document; (3) escalation policies enforced in incident management tooling (PagerDuty, OpsGenie) that auto-escalate if a tier does not acknowledge within its SLA; (4) a severity-to-tier mapping that determines which tier should be paged first for each incident type.

Runbooks are tightly coupled to the escalation contact structure: each tier's contacts must know which runbooks are relevant to them, and each runbook must specify the escalation criteria that trigger the next tier. A contact registry without runbooks leaves responders without guidance; runbooks without enforced escalation contacts leave incidents unrouted when the primary responder cannot resolve.

Agentic AI incidents do not fit cleanly into the incident taxonomy that most SRE teams have built their escalation structures around. A traditional infrastructure incident — database overload, network partition — has a well-understood diagnosis and remediation path. An agent misbehaviour incident may look like a business logic bug (the agent took the wrong action), a model regression (the LLM is producing worse outputs after a provider-side change), a prompt injection attack (a user is manipulating the agent), or a capacity issue (the agent is timing out because the LLM provider is throttling). Each root cause requires different expertise and a different responder.

Without a tiered escalation structure, the first responder receives an alert and must simultaneously diagnose the technical category of the incident, decide who to call, find their contact details, and begin triage. Under production pressure and cognitive load, this leads to delayed escalation, wrong expert paged, or triage time wasted on diagnosis that the right expert could have done in two minutes. The tiered structure separates these concerns: the structure decides who to page and when; the responder focuses on triage and remediation.

For regulated industries, documented escalation paths are also a compliance requirement. Financial services regulators require evidence that operational incidents are escalated to accountable senior personnel within defined time windows. A YAML-configured escalation policy in PagerDuty produces this evidence automatically; manual escalation decisions do not.
The auto-escalation policy pages the next tier if the current tier does not resolve within the SLA, without requiring the current responder to manually decide when to escalate.

Trigger-type-based escalation routes LLM provider failures to the SRE tier, safety violations to the ML engineering and security tier, and vendor SLA breaches to VP level — matching incident type to expert.
Every incident page includes a runbook link, ensuring the first responder has procedural guidance before they begin triage, reducing time-to-first-action.
Incident management tooling records who was paged, when they acknowledged, what actions they took, and when escalation occurred — producing the compliance evidence that manual escalation cannot.
Responders focus on triage and remediation rather than contact-finding and escalation judgment under production pressure.

Contact registries go stale as teams change. An escalation policy that pages someone who left the team six months ago delays incident response by the time it takes to discover the error and find the current contact.
Escalation SLA windows that are too short cause unnecessary escalation of incidents that Tier 1 would have resolved. Windows that are too long delay expert involvement in incidents that require it.
Trigger types that lack runbooks leave the first responder without procedural guidance. The trigger-to-runbook mapping must be maintained as new failure modes are discovered.
The escalation policy configuration in incident management tooling can drift from the YAML source of truth if changes are made directly in the UI. Changes must be applied via infrastructure-as-code.

The escalation structure operates through two mechanisms: the incident management platform (PagerDuty or OpsGenie) enforces the contact sequence and SLA timers automatically; the runbook registry provides the procedural reference that the first responder accesses through the incident alert.

When a monitoring alert fires, the alerting system creates an incident in the incident management platform and pages Tier 1 via the primary on-call rotation. If the Tier 1 responder does not acknowledge within 5 minutes, the platform auto-escalates to a secondary on-call. If neither acknowledges within 15 minutes, the platform escalates to Tier 2 automatically. If Tier 2 does not resolve within 30 minutes, the platform escalates to Tier 3. For P0 incidents (confirmed customer data impact or full service outage), the escalation policy pages Tier 4 immediately alongside Tier 1, regardless of progress.

The runbook link in the incident alert is computed from the alert's trigger type field, which maps to a runbook slug in the runbook registry. The first responder clicks the link and follows the triage steps for the specific trigger type, with embedded escalation criteria that tell them when to manually escalate rather than waiting for the auto-escalation timer.

attrs.alt: Four-tier escalation contact structure: on-call engineer through VP/Legal/Vendor, with time-bounded auto-escalation

content.text: Tiers escalate sequentially with time-bounded auto-escalation enforced by the incident management platform. P0 incidents bypass Tier 1 and page Tier 3 and Tier 4 simultaneously. Dashed arrows represent auto-escalation paths triggered if the current tier does not resolve within its SLA window.

Tiered escalation contact structures apply to any agentic AI system operating in production with availability or safety SLAs. The tier count and SLA windows should reflect the actual escalation complexity: a small team with three engineers might have two tiers with informal SLAs; a regulated enterprise deployment with a 24x7 on-call program needs four tiers with documented SLAs and auto-escalation enforced in tooling.

The structure is not useful without the operational discipline to keep it current. A team that cannot commit to maintaining the contact registry and runbooks — updating contacts when engineers change roles, adding runbooks when new failure modes are discovered, testing the escalation path monthly — will find that the structure erodes and fails at the moment it is needed most.
Step 1: Define the contact registry as code

# config/escalation-contacts.yaml
# Consumed by PagerDuty policy and incident management tooling

tiers:
  - tier: 1
    name: "On-Call Engineer"
    sla_minutes: 5
    pagerduty_schedule: "P_ONCALL_PRIMARY"
    contact_methods: [pagerduty, slack_channel="#incidents"]
    auto_escalate_after_minutes: 15

  - tier: 2
    name: "Senior SRE / Technical Lead"
    sla_minutes: 10
    pagerduty_schedule: "P_SRE_SENIOR"
    contact_methods: [pagerduty, phone_call, slack_channel="#sre-escalation"]
    auto_escalate_after_minutes: 30
    specialisations:
      - llm_provider_issues
      - agent_behaviour_regression
      - capacity_emergencies

  - tier: 3
    name: "ML Engineering Lead + Security"
    sla_minutes: 15
    pagerduty_schedule: "P_ML_LEAD"
    contact_methods: [pagerduty, phone_call]
    auto_escalate_after_minutes: 60
    trigger_conditions:
      - safety_violation_detected
      - novel_attack_pattern
      - data_classification_breach
      - model_output_harm_threshold_exceeded

  - tier: 4
    name: "VP Engineering + Legal + Vendor"
    sla_minutes: 20
    contact_methods: [phone_call, emergency_email]
    trigger_conditions:
      - customer_data_breach
      - regulatory_notification_required
      - p0_sustained_outage_30min
      - llm_vendor_sla_breach
    vendor_escalation:
      anthropic:
        support_email: "support@anthropic.com"
        priority_access: "enterprise_priority_queue"

# runbooks/agent-high-error-rate.md (rendered as inline reference)

## Runbook: Agent High Error Rate (P2)

### Trigger conditions
- Error rate > 5% on /v1/agent/chat for > 5 minutes
- PagerDuty alert: AGENT_ERROR_RATE_HIGH

### Tier 1 Triage Steps

1. **Check LLM provider status** (30 sec)
   curl https://status.anthropic.com/api/v2/summary.json | jq '.components[]
   | select(.name | test("API")) | {name, status}'

2. **Check error distribution by type** (1 min)
   # In Datadog:
   sum:agent.errors{env:prod} by {error_type}.as_count()
   # Look for: model_overloaded, context_length_exceeded, tool_call_timeout

3. **Check recent deployments** (1 min)
   git log --oneline --since="2 hours ago" origin/main

4. **Recovery actions by error type**
   - model_overloaded: enable fallback model, page Tier 2
   - context_length_exceeded: check if prompt template changed recently
   - tool_call_timeout: check tool service health dashboards
   - auth_error: check credential rotation in vault

### Escalation Criteria (Tier 1 -> Tier 2)
- Cannot determine root cause within 15 minutes
- Error rate > 20% (P1 threshold)
- Any safety-related error type
- Production revenue impact confirmed
Step 3: Automate incident creation with escalation routing

# scripts/escalation/create_incident.py
import anthropic_incident_client as ic  # internal library
import yaml, os

def escalate(
    title: str,
    severity: str,          # p0, p1, p2, p3
    trigger_type: str,      # error_rate, latency, safety, custom
    details: dict,
    initial_tier: int = 1,
) -> dict:
    config = yaml.safe_load(open("config/escalation-contacts.yaml"))
    tiers = {t["tier"]: t for t in config["tiers"]}

    tier = tiers[initial_tier]
    incident = ic.create(
        title=title,
        severity=severity,
        body=details.get("body", ""),
        service="agentic-ai-platform",
        escalation_policy=tier["pagerduty_schedule"],
    )
    ic.add_runbook_link(
        incident["id"],
        runbook_url=runbook_url_for_trigger(trigger_type),
    )
    return {
        "incident_id": incident["id"],
        "tier": initial_tier,
        "escalation_policy": tier["pagerduty_schedule"],
        "auto_escalate_at_minutes": tier.get("auto_escalate_after_minutes"),
    }

def runbook_url_for_trigger(trigger_type: str) -> str:
    base = "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/agentic-ai/"
    mapping = {
        "error_rate": "agent-high-error-rate",
        "latency": "agent-high-latency",
        "safety": "agent-safety-violation",
        "llm_provider": "llm-provider-outage",
        "capacity": "capacity-emergency",
    }
    return base + mapping.get(trigger_type, "general-incident")
Step 4: Test runbook coverage and contact reachability

A minimal escalation setup for a two-person engineering team running an internal-only agentic AI tool: Tier 1 is the on-call engineer (both engineers alternate weekly), runbooks cover the three most common failure modes (LLM API rate limit, tool service unavailability, high error rate), and escalation to Tier 2 (the other engineer) happens after 30 minutes. PagerDuty is configured to auto-escalate. The contact registry YAML is version-controlled and applied via Terraform.

A four-tier structure for a financial services firm running a customer-facing document analysis agent: Tier 1 is the 24x7 SRE on-call (6-person rotation); Tier 2 is the senior SRE and AI engineering lead; Tier 3 is the CISO and ML engineering director; Tier 4 is the Chief Technology Officer, General Counsel, and the LLM vendor's enterprise support line. Runbooks exist for 23 trigger types. Escalation SLA windows are 5, 15, 30, and 60 minutes respectively. The entire policy is maintained as Terraform resources applied through a CI pipeline with change review.

A logistics company operating a shipment routing agent experienced a novel failure mode: the LLM provider released a model update that changed the output format of structured JSON responses, causing the agent's tool-call parser to throw exceptions on every request. The incident was initially misclassified as a tool service failure (Tier 1 scope) because the error message pointed to the parser, not the LLM.

Tier 1 spent 22 minutes diagnosing before escalating to Tier 2 (ML engineering), who recognised the output format change within 4 minutes by diffing recent model responses against the expected schema. Total time-to-resolution was 37 minutes. The post-incident review identified that the escalation criteria for the high-error-rate runbook did not include a step to check for LLM provider model version changes, and that the threshold for auto-escalation to Tier 2 (30 minutes) was too long for this type of incident.

The outcome: the runbook was updated to include an LLM provider model version check as step 2 in the high-error-rate triage sequence, and the auto-escalation threshold for incidents involving structured output parsing errors was reduced to 10 minutes. These changes were codified in the escalation contact YAML and the runbook repository within three days of the incident.
Keeping the escalation contact registry in a wiki or spreadsheet means it is not subject to change review and goes stale without anyone noticing.
Runbooks not linked from incident alerts:
A runbook that the responder must search for under production pressure is nearly as useless as no runbook. The runbook link must be embedded in the alert.
Generic escalation path for all incident types:
Routing all incidents through the same tier sequence regardless of type means safety violations get the same first responder as database latency spikes — expertise mismatch that delays resolution.

Escalating to VP level or Legal at 3 AM is appropriate for P0 incidents, but those individuals must be pre-briefed that they are in the escalation path and what their role is. Surprising a VP with a production page without prior context is ineffective.
Contact registries and PagerDuty configurations that are never tested in a non-production scenario will fail in ways that are only discovered during a real incident.
Maintain escalation contact YAML in version control with code review for changes. Apply to PagerDuty/OpsGenie via Terraform or the platform API from CI.
Match trigger type to specialist tier:
Define trigger type categories (LLM provider, agent behaviour, safety, capacity, security) and map each to the tier with the relevant expertise, not just the tier with the next available responder.
Embed runbook links in every alert:
Configure alerting templates to include the runbook URL for each trigger type. The runbook should be reachable in one click from the PagerDuty incident page.
Run a game-day exercise where a simulated incident is created and the full escalation path is exercised. Verify that auto-escalation timers fire, contacts receive pages, and runbooks are accessible.
Update runbooks within 48 hours of each incident:
Every incident that reveals a gap in the runbook — a triage step that was missing, an escalation criterion that was wrong — must be remediated in the runbook before the next on-call rotation.

The escalation structure does not have a performance profile in the computational sense, but it has a load profile: the number of incidents per week, the distribution across tiers, and the resolution time per tier. These metrics determine whether the structure is correctly calibrated.

A healthy escalation distribution has most incidents resolved at Tier 1 (60-70% of all incidents). A distribution where 40% of incidents reach Tier 2 or higher indicates either that Tier 1 runbooks are insufficient, that auto-escalation SLAs are too short, or that the system is experiencing a class of failure that Tier 1 is not equipped to handle. Review the distribution quarterly and adjust runbooks and SLAs accordingly.

As the system scales — more agents, more users, more tool integrations — the incident volume increases. The escalation structure must scale with it by: increasing the Tier 1 on-call rotation size, adding AI-specific specialisation to the Tier 2 rotation (so that ML expertise is available in the first escalation, not just at Tier 3), and adding runbooks for newly discovered failure modes before they reach production.
Alternative to PagerDuty; good Jira integration
Updated by Tier 4 for P0 customer-facing incidents
Must be linked from incident alerts, not just searchable
Alert metadata sets trigger_type used for runbook routing
Auto-creates incident channel with escalation contact list

The escalation contact structure must be reliable under the conditions in which it is most needed: degraded systems, late-night pages, and high cognitive load. Reliability requires: (1) contact information that is up-to-date and accessible even if internal tooling is degraded; (2) runbooks that are hosted on an independent platform that will not be affected by an outage of the system they document; (3) escalation policies that are not dependent on the availability of any single person.
Maintain a static contact list document (PDF or printed) accessible to the on-call engineer even if internal tooling is unavailable. Review and update quarterly.
Host runbooks on a platform independent of the system they document. If the agentic AI platform is down, the runbook must still be accessible.
Configure PagerDuty to use both app push notification and phone call for Tier 2 and above, so that a missed push notification does not delay escalation.
The contact YAML in version control contains phone numbers and personal details. Ensure the repository has appropriate access controls and the file is excluded from public exposure.

A tiered escalation contact structure is a decision tree that has been precomputed, validated, and encoded into tooling so that it runs automatically at 2 AM without requiring the on-call engineer to exercise judgment under pressure. The engineer's mental model when an alert fires should be: open the incident, click the runbook link, follow the triage steps, and trust that the escalation timer will page the next tier automatically if the issue is not resolved.

The structure fails when it requires the responder to make decisions that the structure should have precomputed: who to call, when to escalate, which runbook to use. Any friction point that requires judgment under pressure should be identified and eliminated by making the decision in advance and encoding it in the contact registry or the runbook.
Tiered escalation contact structures must be version-controlled YAML applied to incident management tooling via infrastructure-as-code, not maintained in wikis or spreadsheets.
Agentic AI systems require AI-specific escalation routing: LLM provider failures, safety violations, and agent behaviour regressions each require different specialist expertise at different tiers.
Every incident alert must include a direct link to the relevant runbook, computed from the trigger type metadata, not found through search.
Auto-escalation SLA timers must be enforced by the incident management platform, not by the current responder's manual judgment under production pressure.

The escalation structure must be tested monthly: contact lists go stale, PagerDuty configurations drift, and runbooks become outdated — each is only discovered at the worst moment if not tested routinely.
Runbooks must be updated within 48 hours of each incident that reveals a gap or incorrect triage step, before the gap is encountered again.
